does: This paper introduces JailbreakOPT, a framework that improves jailbreak attacks on language models by combining atomic jailbreak prompts from a tool library through iterative optimization within single-turn interactions. The system frames tool selection as a contextual bandit problem, using contextual Thompson sampling to learn which combinations of jailbreak techniques work best across different attack scenarios. The approach generates stronger standalone attack prompts that achieve higher attack success rates while requiring fewer total attacks to succeed, demonstrating improvements over both hand-crafted single-turn methods and existing iterative optimization approaches across multiple target models and attack objectives.
connects: This work extends prior research on adversarial attacks against language models, building on foundational work in prompt-based jailbreaking that exploits safety training vulnerabilities. It addresses known limitations of existing methods: hand-crafted attacks lack adaptability while iterative mutation-based approaches require excessive queries. The paper connects to broader literature on prompt optimization and few-shot learning, as well as bandit algorithms for efficient exploration-exploitation trade-offs. By organizing attacks into composable atomic prompts and applying contextual Thompson sampling, JailbreakOPT draws on techniques from reinforcement learning and online learning to systematically improve attack strategies without excessive computational cost.
enables: This work establishes new directions for understanding and defending against prompt-based attacks by formalizing jailbreak composition as a learnable optimization problem. It enables future research into more efficient adversarial attack frameworks, including dynamic attack strategies that adapt to specific model behaviors in real-time. The contextual bandit formulation opens possibilities for investigating how attack success correlates with model properties and safety mechanisms. Furthermore, the tool-assisted composition paradigm could inform defensive strategies by revealing how different jailbreak components interact, potentially leading to more robust safety training methods. The framework also suggests pathways toward systematic vulnerability assessment of LLMs under bounded query constraints.
